But being part-time employed and living a somewhat solitary life—my hobbies include classical music but not much socializing—I tend to think things through carefully anyway. response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with discussions about healthcare and COVID vaccines.
response_parsed.opinion:
I'm a nineteen-year-old man living in Kansas City, part-time employed as a carpenter, but I spend my days building things rather than navigating the complex world of online debates. When it comes to talking about healthcare topics like COVID vaccines, I find myself quite overwhelmed because even though I've got parents with one having vocational diplomas and the other a master's degree—wait, that might not be relevant at all—I'm still young and my depression makes these discussions especially difficult for me.

First off, since my neuroticism is sky-high, those conversations about health scares or medical policies can really mess with my head. I remember reading something online one day during my hour of social media use that was just plain scary, making me anxious all afternoon while trying to focus on a blueprint. But then again, being Buddhist and having high openness means I'm not set in stone; I like reading novels where there are different perspectives, so maybe I can approach this with an open mind.

For instance, if someone brings up vaccine hesitations or misinformation, my first reaction might be to get worried because of how sensitive I feel about these topics. But being part-time employed and living a somewhat solitary life—my hobbies include classical music but not much socializing—I tend to think things through carefully anyway. Still, I need strategies that don't require me to jump into endless arguments online.

Perhaps the best way for me is just to listen without feeling like I have to contribute or agree with everyone. That aligns somewhat with my low extraversion and agreeableness scores—though they're both pretty low, meaning social interactions can be draining but I still try not to make enemies—but in practice, it's tough because of the high neuroticism. Also, being from Kansas City where maybe people aren't as into deep discussions as other places? Not sure.

So managing these discussions for me means setting boundaries—like only checking social media briefly and sticking mostly to my hobbies—and avoiding topics that trigger anxiety.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: I'm drawing on my personal experience with depression, high neuroticism, and how these traits affect my ability to engage in stressful discussions. My low openness isn't really true since I have a score of 97—wait no, according to the input it's actually very open but anxious about it—and my Buddhist beliefs suggest tolerance for differing views without needing to debate them extensively.
response_parsed.tone: Cautious and reflective, with underlying anxiety given high neuroticism scores
